key_insight: The paper shows that missing MRI modalities can be generated by treating translation as a joint posterior-sampling problem, enabling flexible reconstruction from whatever modalities are available.
clinical_relevance: This could help produce complete multimodal MRI inputs when some scans are missing, supporting better downstream analysis such as tumor segmentation without requiring retraining for each source-target pair.
method: Learn a joint prior over all MRI modalities with a flow matching model.
Use posterior sampling to enforce consistency with observed modalities while generating missing ones.
Apply a many-to-one sampling strategy to reduce error propagation when generating multiple targets.
evidence_signal: On BraTS and IXI datasets, the method outperformed baselines across most missing-modality settings, and synthesized images improved downstream tumor segmentation performance.
watch_item: Performance depends on accurate measurement consistency and learned priors, so generalization to new scanners, modalities, or highly atypical cases may require careful validation.
paper_title: Posterior Samplings are Missing Modalities Generators for Medical Image Translation
